---
title: "Phase B — Closed-form vs. Monte Carlo validation"
subtitle: "Cross-check the Phase A math, add the tail metrics it cannot give"
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: false
---

```{=html}
<style>
  .metric-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(180px, 1fr)); gap: 0.75rem; margin: 1rem 0; }
  .metric-grid .card { border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 6px; padding: 0.75rem; background: #fafafa; }
  .metric-grid .card .label { font-size: 0.8rem; color: #555; }
  .metric-grid .card .value { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; font-size: 1.2rem; }
  .callout-note-lite { border-left: 3px solid #4c78a8; background: #f3f7fb; padding: 0.6rem 0.9rem; margin: 0.8rem 0; font-size: 0.95rem; }
  .legend-table { font-size: 0.9rem; margin: 0.5rem 0 1rem; }
  .legend-table td:first-child { font-family: ui-monospace, monospace; padding-right: 0.8rem; }
</style>
```

This page has two jobs: confirm the [Phase A](phase-a.qmd) closed-form
moments against Monte Carlo, and add the tail metrics (VaR, CVaR, loss
probability) that closed form cannot give. The `z` column in the §4
scorecard is the numerical sanity check; everything else — histograms,
NAV paths, break-even surfaces — exists to make the comparison and its
consequences easy to read.

The baseline price model is Geometric Brownian Motion (pure GBM plus a
separate jump-diffusion verification in §6). All Monte Carlo
computation happens in TypeScript under `src/`; this page reads the
emitted JSON. To regenerate:

```sh
npm run simulate -- --seed 42
npm run sweep -- --seed 42
```

## Reading notes

Three conventions to keep in mind anywhere you see numbers on this page.

- **The $z$ column is the primary sanity check.** $z = (\mathrm{MC} -
  \mathrm{cf}) / \mathrm{stderr}$. $|z| \leq 2$ is healthy sampling
  variation; $|z| > 3$ points at a simulator bug or too few paths.
- **Variance ratio is algebraic, not statistical.** Fee and
  back-to-back books share the kernel $I_T$, so
  $\mathrm{Var}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]/\mathrm{Var}[R_{\mathrm{fee}}] =
  (P/f)^2$ exactly. Any disagreement with that ratio is a bug, not
  sampling noise.
- **P&L is a horizon total.** USD over $[0, T]$, not annualised and
  not per-tonne. Divide by $N = \lambda T$ for a per-tonne figure.

The fee row's VaR/CVaR are "low end of revenue, not loss" — see the
glossary entry below.

{{< include _glossary.qmd >}}

### Notation

Same conventions as [Phase A](phase-a.qmd) §1–§5.

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
| --- | --- | --- |
| $S_t$ | kVCM spot price at time $t$ | USD / kVCM |
| $P$ | protocol constant (kVCM burned per tonne) | kVCM / tonne |
| $\pi_t := P \cdot S_t$ | USD protocol price per tonne | USD / tonne |
| $\lambda$ | deterministic retirement flow | tonnes / year |
| $T$ | horizon | years |
| $N := \lambda \cdot T$ | total tonnes retired over $[0,T]$ | tonnes |
| $f$ | fee rate | dimensionless |
| $Q$ | fixed USD quote per tonne (principal model) | USD / tonne |
| $\alpha \in [0,1]$ | pre-purchase (hedge) fraction — $0$ back-to-back, $1$ fully matched | dimensionless |
| $\beta \in [0,1]$ | quota-share cession of the $(1-\alpha)$ stochastic leg (§3d) | dimensionless |
| $\theta \geq 0$ | counterparty risk-load multiplier (§3d) | dimensionless |
| $\pi$ | up-front syndication premium | USD |
| $I_T := \int_0^T S_t\,dt$ | area under the price curve — the shared random kernel | (USD/kVCM)·year |
| $R_{\mathrm{fee}} = f \cdot P \cdot \lambda \cdot I_T$ | fee revenue (always $\geq 0$) | USD |
| $\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}} = Q \cdot N - P \cdot \lambda \cdot I_T$ | back-to-back P&L ($\alpha=0$) | USD |
| $\Pi_{\mathrm{matched}} = N \cdot (Q - P \cdot S_0)$ | matched P&L ($\alpha=1$, deterministic) | USD |
| $\Pi_\alpha = (1-\alpha) \cdot \Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}} + \alpha \cdot \Pi_{\mathrm{matched}}$ | partial-hedge P&L | USD |
| $\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}} = \alpha \cdot \Pi_{\mathrm{matched}} + (1-\alpha)(1-\beta) \cdot \Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}} + \pi$ | retained P&L after syndication (§3d) | USD |
| $h \in [1, \infty]$, $H = h \cdot S_0$ | §3e barrier ratio and level; $h=\infty$ disables the switch | dimensionless / USD / kVCM |
| $\tau := \inf\{t : S_t \ge H\} \wedge T$ | §3e first-passage time of $S_t$ to the barrier | years |
| $I_\tau, J_\tau := \int_0^\tau S_t\,dt,\ \int_\tau^T S_t\,dt$ | pre- / post-switch price integrals (§3e); $I_\tau + J_\tau = I_T$ | (USD/kVCM)·year |
| $\Pi_{\mathrm{sw}}^{(1-\alpha)} = Q\lambda\tau - P\lambda I_\tau + f_{\mathrm{post}}P\lambda J_\tau$ | switched stochastic leg (§3e) | USD |
| $Q^* = (1+f) \cdot P \cdot S_0 \cdot (e^{\mu T}-1)/(\mu T)$ | break-even quote equating $\mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}] = \mathbb{E}[R_{\mathrm{fee}}]$ | USD / tonne |

{{< include _alpha-bridge.qmd >}}

## Single-run outputs

One parameter setting, full P&L distributions. Loaded from
`data/run-42.json` (produced by `npm run simulate -- --seed 42`). The
parameter sweep further down is the complementary view: fix the book,
vary the business parameters.


```{ojs}
//| echo: false
run = FileAttachment("data/run-42.json").json()
```

### Run parameters

The scorecard echoes the sliders used for this run. $(\mu, \sigma, T)$
set the price process; $\lambda$ the throughput; $(f, Q, \alpha)$ the
business levers. $Q^*$ is the break-even quote computed from the
others — setting $Q = Q^*$ equalises expected P&L between the fee and
back-to-back books (§5 shows how their *distributions* still differ).

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="metric-grid">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Monte Carlo paths</div><div class="value">${run.params.nPaths.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Time steps / path</div><div class="value">${run.params.nSteps}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">μ (drift, /yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.mu}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">σ (vol, /√yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.sigma}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">T (horizon, yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.T}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">λ (tonnes/yr)</div><div class="value">${run.params.lambda.toLocaleString()}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">f (fee rate)</div><div class="value">${run.params.f}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Q (USD/tonne)</div><div class="value">${"$" + run.params.Q}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">α (hedge ratio)</div><div class="value">${run.params.alpha}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">Q* (break-even)</div><div class="value">${"$" + run.closed.QStar.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
</div>`
```

### §4 — P&L moments: closed-form vs Monte Carlo

The central scorecard. One row per book (`fee`, matched `3a`,
back-to-back `3b`, partial `3c`, retained `3d`, plus `3e` when a
switching barrier is set on this run); one column per statistic. The
left block compares closed-form and MC values — if the simulator is
correct, they agree within $|z| \leq 2$ sampling noise. The `3e` row
reports `NaN` in the closed-form columns because the switching book's
moments have no closed form (see §3e below). The right block reports
the tail metrics that have no closed form.

<div class="legend-table">

| column | meaning |
| --- | --- |
| $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ (cf) / (mc) | expected P&L — closed form vs Monte Carlo |
| $\pm \mathrm{CI}_{95}$ | ±95% half-width of the MC mean ($1.96 \cdot \mathrm{SD}/\sqrt{n}$) |
| SD (cf) / (mc) | standard deviation of P&L |
| $z$ | $(\mathrm{MC} - \mathrm{cf}) / \mathrm{stderr}$ — the primary sanity check |
| $\mathrm{VaR}_{95} / \mathrm{VaR}_{99}$ | loss threshold exceeded with 5% / 1% probability |
| $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95} / \mathrm{CVaR}_{99}$ | mean loss *inside* the worst 5% / 1% tail |
| $\mathbb{P}[\Pi<0]$ | probability of a losing realisation |
| Sharpe | $\mathbb{E}[\Pi] / \mathrm{SD}[\Pi]$ — $f$-invariant for the fee book |

</div>

Row-by-row expectations: `fee` sits entirely in the non-negative
half-plane; `principal_3a` has zero terminal randomness (SD, CI95, $z$
all zero to machine precision); `principal_3b` owns the real loss tail
(read its $\mathrm{CVaR}_{99}$); `principal_3c` should have SD and CVaR
smaller than `3b`'s by exactly $(1-\alpha)$ — the variance collapse
from Phase A §3c; `principal_3d` adds a further $(1-\beta)^2$ variance
collapse via quota-share syndication, invariant in mean at $\theta = 0$.
`principal_3e` (if present) bounds the loss tail by the fee book on
$[\tau, T]$ — see §3e below.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(run.rows, {
  columns: ["name","closedFormMean","mcMean","mcCi95","closedFormSd","mcSd","zScore","var95","var99","cvar95","cvar99","probLoss","sharpe"],
  header: {
    name: "model",
    closedFormMean: "E[Π] (cf)",
    mcMean: "E[Π] (mc)",
    mcCi95: "±CI95",
    closedFormSd: "SD (cf)",
    mcSd: "SD (mc)",
    zScore: "z",
    var95: "VaR95",
    var99: "VaR99",
    cvar95: "CVaR95",
    cvar99: "CVaR99",
    probLoss: "P[Π<0]",
    sharpe: "Sharpe"
  },
  format: {
    closedFormMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mcMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mcCi95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    closedFormSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    mcSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    zScore: x => (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    var99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar95: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    cvar99: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    probLoss: x => x.toFixed(3),
    sharpe: x => x === null ? "—" : (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(3)
  }
})
```

### §6 — Compensated Merton: closed-form means survive jumps

Under the compensated Merton overlay ([Phase A](phase-a.qmd) §6), the
drift term absorbs $-\lambda_J \kappa$ with $\kappa = e^{\mu_J +
\sigma_J^2/2} - 1$, so $\mathbb{E}[S_t] = S_0 e^{\mu t}$ regardless of
$(\lambda_J, \mu_J, \sigma_J)$. Every GBM closed-form mean carries
over; variances and tail metrics do not. The table below re-runs
the simulator under a canonical overlay ($\lambda_J = 3$/yr, $\mu_J =
-0.1$, $\sigma_J = 0.15$) and compares its MC means to the **GBM**
closed form. Expected signature: $|z| \lesssim 2$ on every row (means
agree) with **SD (merton)** strictly above **SD (gbm-cf)** on every
row (jumps firing). Persistent large $|z|$ points at broken
compensation; missing SD widening points at an inert overlay.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(run.jumpCheck.rows, {
  columns: ["name","gbmClosedMean","mertonMcMean","mertonMcCi95","gbmClosedSd","mertonMcSd","zVsGbmClosed"],
  header: {
    name: "model",
    gbmClosedMean: "E[Π] gbm-cf",
    mertonMcMean: "E[Π] merton-mc",
    mertonMcCi95: "±CI95",
    gbmClosedSd: "SD gbm-cf",
    mertonMcSd: "SD merton-mc",
    zVsGbmClosed: "z vs gbm-cf"
  },
  format: {
    gbmClosedMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mertonMcMean: x => (x >= 0 ? "$" : "−$") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
    mertonMcCi95: x => "±$" + x.toFixed(2),
    gbmClosedSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    mertonMcSd: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
    zVsGbmClosed: x => (x >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(x).toFixed(2),
  }
})
```

_Overlay used: $\lambda_J = 3$/yr, $\mu_J = -0.1$, $\sigma_J = 0.15$
(set by the CLI's `JUMP_CHECK` constant). Matching means is the
numerical signature of the compensation identity
$\mathbb{E}[S_t] = S_0 e^{\mu t}$; widened SDs are the signature that
jumps are actually firing._

Spot-check on the same run:

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
html`<div class="metric-grid">
  <div class="card"><div class="label">E[S_T] gbm closed</div><div class="value">${run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.gbmClosed.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">E[S_T] merton mc</div><div class="value">${run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.mertonMcMean.toFixed(4)}</div></div>
  <div class="card"><div class="label">z vs gbm closed</div><div class="value">${(run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.zVsGbmClosed >= 0 ? "" : "−") + Math.abs(run.jumpCheck.terminalSCheck.zVsGbmClosed).toFixed(2)}</div></div>
</div>`
```

### P&L distributions

One panel per stochastic book. The x-axis is terminal P&L in USD; the
solid black rule is closed-form $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ (a converged MC
lands on it); the dashed grey rule is the break-even line at $\Pi =
0$. Mass to the left of the grey rule is the loss probability
$\mathbb{P}[\Pi < 0]$ reported in the table.

- **Fee.** Non-negative by construction, right-skewed (inherits
  $I_T$'s right skew).
- **Principal 3b.** Mirror of the fee panel — same kernel $I_T$ with
  opposite sign. Upside capped at $Q \cdot N$ (token collapses to
  zero); downside unbounded (token rallies). The left-skewed loss
  tail is the central risk concern of the programme.
- **Principal 3c** (partial, $\alpha = 0.5$). Same shape as 3b but
  narrower by $(1-\alpha) = 0.5$ on the SD scale — the visible
  signature of pre-buying inventory.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
pnlData = [
  ...run.sampleTraces.fee.map(v => ({model: "fee", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.b2b.map(v => ({model: "principal_3b", pnl: v})),
  ...run.sampleTraces.partial.map(v => ({model: "principal_3c", pnl: v}))
]
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 420,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginRight: 30,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "terminal P&L Π  (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  y: { label: "empirical density", labelAnchor: "center" },
  fy: { label: "model" },
  color: { legend: true, domain: ["fee","principal_3b","principal_3c"], label: "model" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.ruleX([0], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.rectY(pnlData, Plot.binX({ y: "proportion" }, { x: "pnl", fill: "model", fy: "model", thresholds: 60 })),
    Plot.ruleX(run.rows.filter(r => ["fee","principal_3b","principal_3c"].includes(r.name)),
      { x: "closedFormMean", fy: "name", stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Solid black rule on each panel: closed-form $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$. Dashed grey rule:
break-even $\Pi = 0$._

### $I_T$ histogram vs. Dufresne closed-form moments

$I_T$ is the single random variable that drives every stochastic book
on this page: fee is linear in $I_T$, back-to-back is affine in $I_T$,
partial is an affine mix. So it's worth looking at directly. Dufresne
(2001) gives closed-form mean and variance; $I_T$ is **not** log-normal
despite $S_t$ being so, so its tail quantiles come from Monte Carlo.

The solid black rule is $\mathbb{E}[I_T] = S_0 \cdot (e^{\mu T} -
1)/\mu$; the dashed rules are $\mathbb{E}[I_T] \pm \mathrm{SD}[I_T]$. A
converged MC puts ~68% of the mass between the dashed rules.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
itHist = {
  const { edges, counts } = run.itSampleHistogram;
  const total = counts.reduce((a,b) => a+b, 0);
  return counts.map((c,i) => ({
    x0: edges[i], x1: edges[i+1], density: c / (total * (edges[i+1]-edges[i]))
  }));
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "I_T = ∫₀ᵀ S_t dt   ((USD/kVCM)·year)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36 },
  y: { label: "empirical density", labelAnchor: "center" },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(itHist, { x1: "x0", x2: "x1", y: "density", fill: "#4c78a8", fillOpacity: 0.7 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.closed.IT.mean], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.closed.IT.mean - Math.sqrt(run.closed.IT.variance),
                run.closed.IT.mean + Math.sqrt(run.closed.IT.variance)],
               { stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "3 3" })
  ]
})
```

_Solid black: $\mathbb{E}[I_T]$ (Dufresne closed form). Dashed black: $\mathbb{E}[I_T] \pm \mathrm{SD}[I_T]$._

### Sample GBM paths — matched-book NAV view (§3a)

The matched book's *terminal* P&L is deterministic, but its
mark-to-market inventory value is not. Phase A writes that value as

$$
V_t = (N \cdot P) \cdot (1 - t/T) \cdot S_t
$$

— (tokens still in inventory) × (current spot). Every path pins to
zero at $t = T$ (the burn schedule finishes the inventory), but a
price crash in the middle can carve out a painful interim dip. The
plot shows sampled $V_t$ trajectories (orange = first path, grey =
the rest); the next plot quantifies the worst interim dip across
paths.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
pathRecords = run.sampledPaths.flatMap((path, idx) =>
  path.map((s, k) => ({
    t: k / run.params.nSteps,
    S: s,
    V: run.params.lambda * run.params.T * run.params.P * (1 - k / run.params.nSteps) * s,
    id: idx
  }))
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 900,
  height: 380,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "normalised time  t / T", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "V_t  =  N·P·(1−t/T)·S_t    (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(pathRecords, { x: "t", y: "V", z: "id", stroke: "#888", strokeOpacity: 0.3 }),
    Plot.line(pathRecords.filter(r => r.id === 0), { x: "t", y: "V", stroke: "#c94", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Grey lines: sampled Monte Carlo NAV paths. Orange line: the first path,
highlighted for visibility. $V_T$ is pinned to zero by the burn schedule
regardless of $S_T$._

### NAV shortfall vs. schedule (§3a)

The matched book's solvency question is: *"how far below the
flat-price schedule did NAV dip?"* Formally,

$$
D := \max_{t \leq T} \left[ N \cdot P \cdot (1 - t/T) \cdot (S_0 - S_t) \right]_+
$$

— only the below-schedule excursions count ($[x]_+ = \max(x, 0)$); an
upward excursion is a paper gain, not a solvency event. The histogram
is the distribution of $D$ across simulated paths. The dashed black
rule is the 95th percentile (breached ~1 year in 20); the solid black
rule is the 99th (~1 year in 100). These are the natural capital-
buffer thresholds — the matched book has deterministic terminal P&L
but is not "risk-free" in any operational sense.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 70,
  marginBottom: 50,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "max_t [N·P·(1−t/T)·(S_0−S_t)]₊   (USD shortfall)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  y: { label: "number of paths", labelAnchor: "center" },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(run.sampleTraces.navDrawdown, Plot.binX({y:"count"}, {x: d => d, fill:"#e45", fillOpacity: 0.8})),
    Plot.ruleX([run.drawdown.var95], { stroke: "black", strokeDasharray: "3 3", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.ruleX([run.drawdown.var99], { stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 2 })
  ]
})
```

_Dashed black: 95th percentile of the shortfall distribution. Solid black:
99th percentile. These are the natural stress thresholds for the matched
book even though its terminal P&L is deterministic._

## Parameter sweep

The single-run view above fixes all parameters. This section instead
fixes the book (principal 3c) and a single risk summary
($\mathrm{CVaR}_{95}$), and sweeps the business parameters $(\alpha,
\mu, \sigma)$. Larger $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95}$ is worse. Data from
`data/sweep.json`; slice it with the controls below.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
sweep = FileAttachment("data/sweep.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickAlpha = Inputs.range([0,1], { step: 0.25, value: 0.5, label: "hedge ratio α" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickMu = Inputs.select(sweep.grid.mus, { value: 0.05, label: "drift μ (/yr)" })
```

### Tail risk vs volatility $\sigma$ (fixed $\alpha, \mu$)

At the selected $(\alpha, \mu)$, how does $\mathrm{CVaR}_{95}$ scale
with $\sigma$? More volatility widens $\mathrm{Var}[I_T]$, fattens
the principal loss tail, and inflates CVaR — so the curve should be
monotonically increasing. A flat or non-monotone line is a bug.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
filteredBySigma = sweep.cells.filter(c => c.alpha === pickAlpha && c.mu === pickMu)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "volatility σ   (annualised)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "linear" },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of Π (principal 3c)   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(filteredBySigma, { x: "sigma", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, stroke: "#c53", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(filteredBySigma, { x: "sigma", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, fill: "#c53", r: 4 })
  ]
})
```

_Sensitivity of the 3c tail to $\sigma$ at the selected $(\alpha, \mu)$. A monotone rise
is the expected signature — vol directly inflates $\mathrm{Var}[I_T]$ and hence the
tail of $\Pi_\alpha$._

### Tail risk vs hedge ratio $\alpha$ (fixed $\mu$, $\sigma$ at grid[1])

The dose-response curve for pre-buying. $\sigma$ is held at the
second grid value (middle-of-the-road) while $\alpha$ sweeps from 0
(back-to-back) to 1 (fully matched). Phase A §3c gives
$\mathrm{SD}[\Pi_\alpha] = (1-\alpha) \cdot
\mathrm{SD}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]$, so the CVaR curve should collapse
roughly linearly toward zero. At $\alpha = 1$ the book is
deterministic and CVaR resolves to the sign of the matched P&L (a
single value, not a tail).

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
byAlpha = sweep.cells.filter(c => c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === sweep.grid.sigmas[1])
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Hedge ratio α vs tail risk. Fixed μ (selected above), σ = second grid value. At α = 1 the book is deterministic so CVaR95 collapses to the sign of the matched P&L.",
  x: { label: "hedge ratio α   (0 = back-to-back, 1 = fully matched)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of Π (principal 3c)   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(byAlpha, { x: "alpha", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, stroke: "#35a", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(byAlpha, { x: "alpha", y: d => d.partial.cvar95, fill: "#35a", r: 4 })
  ]
})
```

## §3d — Quota-share syndication ($\beta$)

Phase A §3d adds a second dimension $\beta \in [0, 1]$ — the fraction of
the $(1 - \alpha)$ stochastic leg ceded to a third-party counterparty
for an up-front premium $\pi$. The retained P&L

$$
\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}} = \mathbb{E}[\Pi_\alpha] + (1 - \alpha)(1 - \beta) \cdot (\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}} - \mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}])
$$

(at the fair premium, $\theta = 0$) is an affine rescaling of
$\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}$ with slope $(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)$ around
$\mathbb{E}[\Pi_\alpha]$. The figures below derive the reshape from the
sweep cells' $\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}$ statistics — **no new Monte Carlo is
needed** because the affine identity pushes every quantile, CVaR, and
mean through a closed-form map. The Gaussian CVaR shape factor used by
the `cvar` premium mode is $\phi(\Phi^{-1}(0.95))/0.05 \approx 2.063$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickSigma = Inputs.select(sweep.grid.sigmas, { value: sweep.grid.sigmas[1], label: "volatility σ" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickTheta = Inputs.range([0, 1], { step: 0.05, value: 0, label: "counterparty load θ" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof pickMode = Inputs.radio(["sharpe", "cvar"], { value: "sharpe", label: "premium mode" })
```

### Retained CVaR₉₅ vs $\beta$ across hedge ratios $\alpha$

At the selected $(\mu, \sigma, \theta)$, the retained-book tail collapses
linearly with $(1-\beta)$ for each $\alpha$ — the two axes multiply into
$(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)$, so the top and bottom lines of this plot are
$\alpha = 0$ (back-to-back, no internal hedge) and $\alpha = 1$ (fully
matched, deterministic before syndication). Moving $\theta$ up shifts
every curve *downward* by $\beta(1-\alpha) \cdot \rho(\theta)$ — the
risk premium the intermediary pays the counterparty.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
gaussianCvar95 = 2.062713055949736
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
betaGrid = Array.from({ length: 21 }, (_, i) => i / 20)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
retainedCvarCurves = {
  // E[Π_α] = α · matched + (1-α) · E[Π_b2b]; at θ=0 the cession is mean-preserving.
  // CVaR95(Π_ret) = (1-α)(1-β)·cvar95(b2b) − (1-α)·β·mean(b2b) − α·matched
  //                 + β(1-α)·loadFactor·sd(b2b)·θ   (θ load is an extra loss to the
  //                 intermediary and therefore adds to -E[Π_ret]).
  const loadFactor = pickMode === "cvar" ? gaussianCvar95 : 1;
  const rows = [];
  for (const alpha of sweep.grid.alphas) {
    for (const beta of betaGrid) {
      const cell = sweep.cells.find(c => c.alpha === alpha && c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === pickSigma);
      if (!cell) continue;
      const b2bMean = cell.b2b.mean;
      const b2bCvar = cell.b2b.cvar95;
      const b2bSd = cell.b2b.sd;
      const matchedMean = cell.matched.mean;
      // Identity derived from Π_ret = E[Π_α] + (1-α)(1-β)·(Π_b2b − E[Π_b2b]) + (π_loaded − π_fair).
      // The last term is −β(1-α)·θ·loadFactor·sd(b2b); it translates the distribution.
      const fairCvar = (1 - alpha) * (1 - beta) * b2bCvar
                      - (1 - alpha) * beta * b2bMean
                      - alpha * matchedMean;
      const loadShift = beta * (1 - alpha) * pickTheta * loadFactor * b2bSd;
      rows.push({ alpha, beta, cvar: fairCvar + loadShift });
    }
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 380,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Retained CVaR₉₅ at the selected (μ, σ, θ). Each line is one α from the sweep grid; β sweeps 0→1. The collapse is linear in (1-β) for every α, and θ > 0 shifts curves upward (more tail loss) because the intermediary pays away mean.",
  x: { label: "cession fraction β", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of Π_ret   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  color: { legend: true, label: "hedge ratio α", domain: sweep.grid.alphas, scheme: "viridis", type: "ordinal" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(retainedCvarCurves, { x: "beta", y: "cvar", stroke: "alpha", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

### Fair vs loaded premium across retention

At fixed $\alpha = 0$ (pure back-to-back) the fair premium is
$\pi_{\mathrm{fair}} = \beta \cdot \mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}]$ —
linear in $\beta$. Raising $\theta$ carves the loaded premium downward
proportionally to $\beta \cdot \rho(\theta)$; the gap between the two
curves is the risk premium the counterparty extracts.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
premiumCurves = {
  const loadFactor = pickMode === "cvar" ? gaussianCvar95 : 1;
  const cell0 = sweep.cells.find(c => c.alpha === 0 && c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === pickSigma);
  if (!cell0) return [];
  const thetaLevels = [0, 0.25, 0.5, 1];
  const rows = [];
  for (const theta of thetaLevels) {
    for (const beta of betaGrid) {
      const piFair = beta * cell0.b2b.mean;
      const piLoaded = piFair - beta * theta * loadFactor * cell0.b2b.sd;
      rows.push({ theta: `θ = ${theta}`, beta, pi: piLoaded, isFair: theta === 0 });
    }
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 360,
  marginLeft: 90,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Premium paid to the intermediary as a function of β, for a handful of θ values. θ = 0 is the actuarially fair premium (the straight blue line). Larger θ carves the curve down — the intermediary surrenders expected P&L to tighten the retained tail.",
  x: { label: "cession fraction β", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "premium π   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", zero: true, tickFormat: "$~s" },
  color: { legend: true, label: "load θ" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(premiumCurves, { x: "beta", y: "pi", stroke: "theta", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

### Expected-P&L cost of syndication

At $\theta = 0$ the mean is invariant in $\beta$ — that is the "no free
lunch" identity. At $\theta > 0$ the intermediary pays the counterparty
a premium strictly below fair; the shortfall appears directly on its
expected P&L. This plot is $\mathbb{E}[\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}}] -
\mathbb{E}[\Pi_\alpha]$ at $\alpha = 0$ as a function of $\beta$, one
curve per load level.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
meanShiftCurves = {
  const loadFactor = pickMode === "cvar" ? gaussianCvar95 : 1;
  const cell0 = sweep.cells.find(c => c.alpha === 0 && c.mu === pickMu && c.sigma === pickSigma);
  if (!cell0) return [];
  const thetaLevels = [0, 0.25, 0.5, 1];
  const rows = [];
  for (const theta of thetaLevels) {
    for (const beta of betaGrid) {
      const shift = -beta * theta * loadFactor * cell0.b2b.sd;
      rows.push({ theta: `θ = ${theta}`, beta, shift });
    }
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 90,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Expected-P&L cost of syndication as a function of β, at α = 0. The θ = 0 curve is the flat no-free-lunch line at zero; larger θ carves linearly downward.",
  x: { label: "cession fraction β", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, domain: [0, 1] },
  y: { label: "E[Π_ret] − E[Π_α]   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: "$~s" },
  color: { legend: true, label: "load θ" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.line(meanShiftCurves, { x: "beta", y: "shift", stroke: "theta", strokeWidth: 2 }),
  ]
})
```

## §3e — Switching strategy ($h$)

§3c and §3d **rescale** the loss tail (multiply it by $(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)$);
§3e is the first primitive that **truncates** it. The intermediary operates
the §3b back-to-back book normally, but the first time $S_t$ crosses an upper
barrier $H = h \cdot S_0$ (stopping time $\tau$), every subsequent retirement
flow is paid at the fee rate $f_{\mathrm{post}}$ rather than at the fixed
quote $Q$. The stochastic leg becomes

$$
\Pi_{\mathrm{sw}}^{(1-\alpha)} = Q \, \lambda \, \tau - P \, \lambda \, I_\tau + f_{\mathrm{post}} \, P \, \lambda \, J_\tau
$$

and composes with §3c ($\alpha$) and §3d ($\beta$) exactly as $\Pi_{\mathrm{b2b}}$
did — three orthogonal levers: capital, counterparty, market timing. Because
$\tau$ is a stopping time rather than a path-average, there is no closed-form
P&L density; Monte Carlo is authoritative. Under pure GBM the Harrison (1985)
first-passage distribution anchors $\mathbb{P}[\tau \le T]$ and $\mathbb{E}[\tau \wedge T]$
(test oracles); Merton jumps invalidate both anchors but raise the switch
probability by punching through the barrier in one step.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
switchingSweep = FileAttachment("data/switching-sweep.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
switchingRows = switchingSweep.cells.map(c => ({
  // h = Infinity is serialised as null — label it for the x-axis.
  h: c.h === null ? Infinity : c.h,
  hLabel: c.h === null ? "∞ (no switch)" : c.h.toFixed(2),
  mean: c.row.mean,
  cvar95: c.row.cvar95,
  cvar99: c.row.cvar99,
  probLoss: c.row.probLoss,
  probSwitch: c.switching ? c.switching.probSwitch.mc : 0,
  expectedTau: c.switching ? c.switching.expectedTau.mc : switchingSweep.base.T,
  meanFracInFeeMode: c.switching ? c.switching.meanFracInFeeMode : 0,
  retainedMean: c.retained.mean,
  retainedCvar95: c.retained.cvar95,
  feeMean: c.fee.mean,
  feeCvar95: c.fee.cvar95
}))
```

### CVaR₉₅ and $\mathbb{E}[\Pi]$ vs barrier $h$

The headline operator-decision chart. Left axis: CVaR₉₅ — tightening the
barrier (lowering $h$) collapses the tail from §3d's wide loss distribution
down toward the fee book's lightly-negative CVaR. Right axis: expected
P&L — tightening the barrier simultaneously lifts the mean (moving losses
into fee-captured revenue). The two lines cross the §3d reference (dashed)
at the "knee" of the trade-off.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 400,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginRight: 60,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "§3e switching book — CVaR₉₅ (USD loss, lower is better) and E[Π] (USD gain, higher is better) as a function of barrier h. Finite h on a log scale; the right-most 'no switch' anchor reproduces §3d. Lower h truncates more of the loss tail but also caps more of the upside by handing flow to the fee book earlier.",
  x: { label: "barrier h = H/S₀   (log)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "log", domain: [1, 3] },
  y: { label: "CVaR95 of Π_{3e}   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "cvar95", stroke: "#c33", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "cvar95", fill: "#c33", r: 4 }),
    Plot.ruleY([switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedCvar95 ?? null], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "4 4" }),
    Plot.text([{x: 2.6, y: switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedCvar95 ?? 0, t: "§3d retained (h=∞)"}],
      { x: "x", y: "y", text: "t", fill: "#666", textAnchor: "end", dy: -8 })
  ]
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 860,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  caption: "Expected P&L across barriers. Tighter h shifts mean upward because the fee book's positive-revenue payoff replaces the b2b book's mean-zero-but-fat-tailed payoff on switched paths.",
  x: { label: "barrier h = H/S₀   (log)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36, type: "log", domain: [1, 3] },
  y: { label: "E[Π_{3e}]   (USD)", labelAnchor: "center", tickFormat: "$~s" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "mean", stroke: "#2a7", strokeWidth: 2 }),
    Plot.dot(switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h)), { x: "h", y: "mean", fill: "#2a7", r: 4 }),
    Plot.ruleY([switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedMean ?? null], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "4 4" }),
    Plot.text([{x: 2.6, y: switchingRows.find(r => !isFinite(r.h))?.retainedMean ?? 0, t: "§3d retained (h=∞)"}],
      { x: "x", y: "y", text: "t", fill: "#666", textAnchor: "end", dy: -8 })
  ]
})
```

### Switch-activation profile

Two diagnostics summarising how the barrier is actually exercised: the
empirical probability of switching over $[0, T]$ and the expected fraction
of the horizon operated in fee mode. Both drop as $h$ increases; both are
zero at $h = \infty$ by definition.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Inputs.table(
  switchingRows.filter(r => isFinite(r.h) || true),
  {
    columns: ["hLabel", "probSwitch", "expectedTau", "meanFracInFeeMode", "mean", "cvar95"],
    header: {
      hLabel: "h",
      probSwitch: "P[τ<T]",
      expectedTau: "E[τ∧T]",
      meanFracInFeeMode: "E[(T−τ)/T]",
      mean: "E[Π_{3e}]",
      cvar95: "CVaR95"
    },
    format: {
      probSwitch: x => x.toFixed(3),
      expectedTau: x => x.toFixed(3),
      meanFracInFeeMode: x => x.toFixed(3),
      mean: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2),
      cvar95: x => "$" + x.toFixed(2)
    }
  }
)
```

## Break-even quote surface $Q^*(\mu, T)$

What per-tonne quote $Q$ equalises *expected* revenue between the
principal and fee books? The answer is
$Q^* = (1 + f) \cdot P \cdot S_0 \cdot (e^{\mu T} - 1)/(\mu T)$.

Divide both sides by $(1+f) \cdot P \cdot S_0$ to strip out the
uninteresting scaling:

$$
\frac{Q^*}{(1+f) \cdot P \cdot S_0} = \frac{e^{\mu T} - 1}{\mu T}.
$$

The dimensionless ratio is the premium (ratio $> 1$) or discount
(ratio $< 1$) the principal operator must charge relative to today's
fee quote, to cover expected drift over the horizon. The dashed rule
at 1.0 marks the $\mu = 0$ limit; $\mu > 0$ curves sit above
(appreciation premium), $\mu < 0$ curves sit below (expected
cheapening). Longer $T$ pushes every non-zero curve further from 1.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
qstar = FileAttachment("data/qstar-surface.json").json()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
qstarRecords = qstar.mus.flatMap((mu, i) =>
  qstar.Ts.map((T, j) => ({ mu, T, Q: qstar.values[i][j] }))
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
Plot.plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 400,
  marginLeft: 80,
  marginRight: 120,
  marginBottom: 55,
  grid: true,
  x: { label: "horizon T   (years)", labelAnchor: "center", labelOffset: 36 },
  y: { label: "Q*  /  ((1+f)·P·S₀)     (dimensionless)", labelAnchor: "center" },
  color: { legend: true, label: "drift μ (/yr)", scheme: "viridis", type: "ordinal" },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([1], { stroke: "#888", strokeDasharray: "2 4" }),
    Plot.line(qstarRecords, {
      x: "T",
      y: d => d.Q / ((1 + run.params.f) * run.params.P * run.params.S0),
      stroke: "mu",
      strokeWidth: 2
    }),
    Plot.dot(qstarRecords, {
      x: "T",
      y: d => d.Q / ((1 + run.params.f) * run.params.P * run.params.S0),
      fill: "mu",
      r: 3
    })
  ]
})
```

_Curves for each drift $\mu$ in the grid. Positive drift lifts $Q^*$ above the
fee-based quote (principal must charge more to compensate for expected
kVCM appreciation); negative drift pushes it below. The $\mu = 0$ curve
collapses onto the dashed unit line._

## Implementation notes

- **Performance.** The Phase C simulator runs sub-second at 5,000
  paths × 100 steps on a typical laptop; the sweep and the jump-check
  MC on this page use the same defaults.
- **Reproducibility.** The PRNG is a seeded Mulberry32
  (`src/rng.ts`). A given `(seed, inputs)` pair is deterministic
  across reloads, both here and in the live Phase C port.
- **Still baseline.** Retirement flow is deterministic. Phase C
  iterations will swap in Poisson demand and regime switching behind
  the same UI; the Merton jump overlay is already live on Phase C.

For the cross-phase synthesis and the deterministic stress-test tool,
see the [Findings](index.qmd) page.
